blocks.content: Eli made it up next.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Oh, dang.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Oh, dang.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Oh, dang.
He said it three times with increasing sincerity, then pointed at his own house — the blue roof his mama had been meaning to repaint since April.

When Daisy came up last, the catwalk was narrower than it had looked from the ground. She moved to stand beside Kit, closer than they would have been anywhere else — railing on one side, the tank behind them, not enough room for real distance between them.
Neither of them mentioned it.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You can see the creek from here.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Yeah.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I didn't know the Henderson property was that close to it.
The overgrown yard with the rusted truck was visible — a rectangle of tall grass inside the residential grid, smaller from sixty feet.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: It looks different from up here.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Everything does.
He wasn't looking at the Henderson property. He was looking south.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: What are you looking at?

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: My mom's street. That oak tree — the big one by the Winstons'. That's how far it is.
Daisy found it. Even from here, the canopy was a dark round mass against the paler green of surrounding yards.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I didn't know you could see it from here.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Yeah.
He said it quietly. Not the way he usually said things.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: We should go back down before Eli tries to stand on the railing.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: I was not going to stand on the railing.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You were thinking about it.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: I was thinking about a lot of things. That was one of them.
